name: Articles — le, la, les, un, une

cards.grammarNote: Used before masculine singular nouns: le café, le livre, le pain. Contracts with à → au and with de → du.
Used before feminine singular nouns: la table, la maison, la ville. No contraction with à or de.

cards.front: the (before a vowel or mute h)
cards.grammarNote: le and la both shorten to l' before a vowel or mute h: l'eau, l'ami, l'hôtel, l'amie. This is called elision.
Used for all plural nouns, regardless of gender: les livres, les tables, les amis. Contracts with à → aux and with de → des.
Indefinite article for masculine singular nouns: un café, un livre, un ami. In negation, changes to de: Je n'ai pas de livre.
Indefinite article for feminine singular nouns: une table, une maison, une pomme. In negation, changes to de.
Plural indefinite article: des livres (some books), des amis (some friends). In negation, changes to de: Je n'ai pas de livres.
Mandatory contraction: à + le = au. Je vais au café. Le prix du café (de + le = du). Never write 'à le'.
Mandatory contraction: à + les = aux. Je parle aux enfants. Also de + les = des (in possession sense).
Nouns ending in -ment, -age, -isme are usually masculine. The gender must often be memorized — look it up when learning new vocabulary.
Nouns ending in -tion, -sion, -ure, -ette, -ance are usually feminine: la nation, la lecture, la boulangette.
In French, general likes/dislikes use the definite article: J'aime le café, J'aime les chats. English omits the article ('I like coffee'), but French does not.

cards.front: I want a coffee. (one specific item)
cards.grammarNote: When ordering or requesting a single item, use the indefinite article: un/une. Je veux un café = I want a (one) coffee.
After negation (ne...pas), un/une/des all change to de (or d' before a vowel). The definite article (le/la/les) does NOT change after negation.

cards.front: She is a doctor. (profession after être)
cards.grammarNote: After être, professions drop the article: Elle est médecin (not 'une médecin'). But if the noun is modified: Elle est une bonne médecin.
Eau is feminine, so 'la eau' would be expected — but the vowel causes elision: l'eau. The gender still matters for agreement: l'eau est froide (fem. adjective).
de + les = des. This des is different from the plural indefinite article des — context makes the meaning clear.
